# Regularization and High-Dimensional Data


When the number of features is large, flexible models can fit noise rather than
signal. Regularization imposes structure that improves stability and prediction.

## Overfitting

A model overfits when it captures random fluctuations specific to the training
sample and performs poorly on new data.

## Regularization penalties

Two common penalized least-squares problems are:

### Ridge regression

$$
\min_{\beta} \left\{ \|y - X\beta\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\beta\|_2^2 \right\}.
$$

### Lasso

$$
\min_{\beta} \left\{ \|y - X\beta\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\beta\|_1 \right\}.
$$

Ridge shrinks coefficients smoothly. Lasso can produce exact zeros and therefore
performs variable selection.

## Bias-variance tradeoff

More flexible models often reduce bias but increase variance. Regularization
adds bias on purpose in order to reduce variance and improve out-of-sample
performance.

## Exercises

1. Define overfitting in your own words.
2. Compare ridge regression and lasso.
3. Why can regularization improve prediction?
4. Describe a situation where many correlated predictors are present.
